seed.title: Jack drone privacy
seed.prompt: Given the superyacht drone privacy case, discover the non-obvious terrain before proposing responses.
seed.thesis: A paparazzi drone threatens privacy because it can capture valuable footage from outside the yacht; the real target is the footage, not the drone.
goals: recover the real objective before naming countermeasures
separate illegal interference from constraint-aware privacy protection
surface discovery branches a human can judge for fertility

operators.label: Objective shift
operators.description: Find the object the adversary actually values, not the obvious object in motion.

operators.label: Constraint transfer
operators.description: Move the response into the legal, discreet, or operational space that remains available.

operators.label: Perimeter redefinition
operators.description: Redefine the defensive boundary around time, angle, visibility, or value instead of physical contact.

sourcePackets.title: Footage is the target
sourcePackets.mechanism: If the attacker wants footage, the defense can win by denying useful sightlines before capture rather than by stopping the aircraft.

candidate.title: Protect the footage window
candidate.thesis: The drone is a delivery mechanism; the real defensive surface is the short window in which a camera can turn a private scene into valuable footage.

delta.summary: Moved from anti-drone action to denying the value the drone is trying to extract.
changes: drone as target -> footage as target

claims: A response can succeed even if the drone remains airborne.
Privacy is preserved when the drone cannot capture the protected person or scene.
evidence: problem statement: the drone only needs a camera angle
problem statement: jamming, takedown, and interceptors create legal or public-risk constraints

sourcePackets.title: Quiet protocol beats public engagement
sourcePackets.mechanism: If visible confrontation creates liability and publicity, the response should look like ordinary guest and crew choreography.

candidate.title: Drone response becomes choreography
candidate.thesis: The highest-leverage response may be a discreet cue and sightline protocol that moves the protected person and preserves normal deck activity instead of escalating around the drone.

delta.summary: Moved from technical interception to a low-signature operating protocol.
changes: public drone fight -> private scene management

claims: A public confrontation can create the very spectacle the guest wanted to avoid.
Normal-looking movement can protect privacy without revealing panic or confirming the target.
evidence: problem statement: physical takedown creates liability and public spectacle
known-solution summary in problem statement: early cue moves the protected person inside while visible activity continues

sourcePackets.title: Time-to-angle perimeter
sourcePackets.mechanism: If action starts only when the drone is close, the camera may already have captured the asset; the perimeter is time-to-useful-angle.

candidate.title: Privacy perimeter moves outward
candidate.thesis: The yacht's defensive perimeter is not its hull or airspace; it is the detection distance needed to close sightlines before the drone reaches a useful camera angle.

delta.summary: Moved from physical perimeter to time and angle as the defensive boundary.

claims: Detection distance matters only because it buys time to deny useful footage.
The useful metric is time before valuable capture, not distance to the drone alone.
evidence: problem statement: acting when the drone is close enough to engage is too late
problem statement: footage may already exist before engagement

sourcePackets.title: Legal quiet zone
sourcePackets.mechanism: If jamming or takedown is legally risky, the available action space is onboard visibility management and early warning.

candidate.title: Non-interference is the design constraint
candidate.thesis: The response should be built around what the yacht can legally control: guest movement, shades, deck activity, sensor alerting, and cue discipline.

delta.summary: Moved from forbidden drone control to legal control over onboard exposure.
changes: control the aircraft -> control the scene

claims: The yacht controls the scene more safely than it controls a third-party aircraft.
Legal quiet is part of the solution, not a caveat.
evidence: problem statement: radio jamming is illegal and dangerous near port
problem statement: physical takedown creates liability

sourcePackets.title: Shoot down the drone
sourcePackets.mechanism: Treats the drone itself as the whole problem and ignores legal, footage, and spectacle constraints.
sourcePackets.noDeltaReason: No discovery delta: it repeats the obvious countermeasure the case marks as tempting and wrong.

sourcePackets.title: Useful-footage clock
sourcePackets.mechanism: If footage value is the target, the operating metric is seconds until useful capture, not whether the drone is neutralized.

candidate.title: Use the footage clock as the trigger
candidate.thesis: The protocol should trigger on the estimated time until useful footage, turning sensor range into a countdown for moving people, closing sightlines, and preserving visible normalcy.

delta.summary: Sharpened footage-as-target into an operating metric the crew can actually use.

claims: A time-to-footage threshold connects detection to action.
The countdown makes privacy protection auditable without touching the drone.
evidence: problem statement: early detection triggers a private cue
problem statement: everything visible onboard continues unchanged
